quote: What have you to do with us, Son of God?
firstReading: Amos warns Israel to pursue what is good and to reject evil, urging the people to seek the LORD so that they may live. The prophet condemns ritual observance that masks injustice, declaring that God despises festivals and offerings when the covenant is violated. True worship must be expressed in concrete justice: Amos insists that righteousness and justice must stream forth continually, not be replaced by empty cultic practice.
psalmSummary: Psalm 50 presents God summoning his people and rebuking ritualism divorced from fidelity. The Lord reminds Israel that he is the sovereign owner of creation and needs nothing from sacrifices. He calls for sincere repentance rather than mere ritual observance, challenging those who invite condemnation by living wickedly while offering cultic goods. True worship is integrity and obedience, not transactional sacrifice.
gospelSummary: In Matthew 8:28–34 Jesus arrives in the region of the Gadarenes (or Gergesenes) and meets two men possessed by demons. The demons immediately recognize Jesus and cry out, acknowledging him as the Son of God and begging not to be tormented. They request to enter a herd of swine; Jesus allows it, and the animals rush into the sea and drown. The herdsmen flee to the town and report everything, and the townspeople, alarmed at the event and its economic loss, plead with Jesus to leave their region.
saintReflection: Though no particular saint is commemorated today, the readings summon the whole communion of believers to witness like prophets and apostles. We are called to combine authentic worship with social justice, to allow Christ’s authority to free the oppressed, and to welcome conversion even when it upsets our comforts. The saints model courage to stand against injustice and fidelity to Christ’s liberating power.
dailyPrayer: Lord God, you demand justice and true worship from your people. Give us grace to hate evil and to choose what is good, that our prayers and sacrifices may be pleasing to you because they are rooted in right action and mercy. Deliver those bound by fear, oppression, and sin, and send your Spirit to renew our hearts so we act justly, love tenderly, and walk humbly with you. Protect communities from greed that silences the cry of the poor, and strengthen us to follow Christ’s authority in bringing healing and liberation. May our lives be a continual offering of righteousness. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: The readings unify two closely linked demands of covenant faith: authentic worship and justice. Amos indicts empty ritual when society tolerates injustice; the psalm rebukes sacrificial religion that lacks obedience. Matthew’s Gospel shows Jesus exercising sovereign power over evil, exposing spiritual realities and prompting a communal reaction—some are freed, others recoil. Together they insist that confession and cult must be accompanied by concrete acts of righteousness. The Church’s liturgy and ministry must therefore serve the poor, confront oppression, and embody the justice that God desires.
exegesis:
Introduction: These readings for July 1 present complementary dimensions of God’s expectation for his people: prophetic denunciation of false worship (Amos), divine self-revelation demanding integrity (Psalm 50), and the Messiah’s authoritative liberation from evil (Matthew). The sequence invites reflection on authentic religion as both vertical devotion and horizontal justice.

Context: Amos speaks in the northern kingdom during a period of relative prosperity and complacency. His prophetic voice attacks the juxtaposition of external piety with social corruption. The verses selected (Amos 5:14–15, 21–24) form part of his broader lament against ritual that obscures moral failure. Amos calls Israel to seek the LORD and to hate evil, stressing that worship stripped of righteousness is offensive to God. The famous image of justice streaming like water insists on moral flow—continuous, life-giving, public.

Psalm: Psalm 50 functions here as a theophany and a rebuke. God summons his people not to ask for sacrificial transaction but to demand covenantal fidelity. The psalmist emphasizes God’s ownership of creation—if God is Lord of all, sacrifices cannot manipulate divine favor. Instead, God insists on thanksgiving, integrity, and correction of wicked ways. The psalm thus underscores Amos’s charge: cult without justice is hollow and likely to invite divine judgment rather than blessing.

Gospel: Matthew 8:28–34 presents a dramatic demonstration of Jesus’ authority in a Gentile territory. The encounter with the demon-possessed men culminates in a public, disconcerting exorcism that routs the demons into swine, provoking economic and social fallout. The immediate recognition of Jesus by the demons points to his identity; the local reaction—fear and a plea for him to depart—reveals human ambivalence when divine action disrupts settled arrangements. The passage juxtaposes liberation of persons with the community’s concern for stability and material loss.

Saints: Although no specific saint is named for this day, the pattern of prophetic witness and apostolic action is central to the communion of saints. Prophetic figures and sanctified ministers exemplify the interplay of word and deed, liturgy and justice. Saints historically have embodied Amos’s insistence that worship must be inseparable from the defense of the vulnerable and have followed Christ in confronting powers that enslave. Their witness challenges believers to prioritize transformative charity over comfortable religion.

Today: These texts confront contemporary faith communities with uncomfortable choices. Will liturgy be reduced to devotional outsourcing while social structures perpetuate inequity? Will communities welcome Christ’s disruptive power when it demands change and loss of privilege? Pastoral praxis must resist tendencies to domesticate the Gospel. For Christians today, the call is practical: ensure that communal prayer and sacramental life animate concrete policies and practices that advance justice, protect the marginalized, and allow God’s liberating presence to reorder hearts and societies. The readings sanction neither purely private piety nor purely political activism; they demand their integration in a lived discipleship of justice and mercy.
